nodes.name: Duets & Supergroups
nodes.description: Iconic musical collaborations and supergroup lineups

items.prompt: Which power trio, formed in London in 1966 and active for only two years, is widely regarded as rock's first supergroup?
items.explanation:
British rock power trio active 1966–1968: Eric Clapton (guitar), Jack Bruce (bass/vocals), Ginger Baker (drums)
Widely regarded as the first rock supergroup — all three had achieved fame independently before joining
Clapton came from the Yardbirds and John Mayall's Bluesbreakers; Bruce and Baker from the Graham Bond Organisation
Hit songs include 'Sunshine of Your Love' and 'White Room' — their only two U.S. top-10 singles
Sold over 15 million records despite a career spanning just over two years
Bruce and Baker famously despised each other — their mutual hostility dated back to the Graham Bond Organisation
Farewell concerts at the Royal Albert Hall in November 1968; briefly reunited there in 2005

items.prompt: Which 1988 supergroup featured a Beatle, a Nobel laureate, and the frontman of the Heartbreakers among its five members?
items.explanation:
Supergroup formed in Los Angeles in 1988: George Harrison, Bob Dylan, Tom Petty, Roy Orbison, Jeff Lynne (ELO)
Originated when Harrison needed a B-side; the jam session at Dylan's Malibu studio produced 'Handle with Care,' deemed too good for a B-side
Members used pseudonyms and claimed to be half-brothers: Nelson, Otis, Lefty, Charlie T. Jr., and Lucky
Debut album *Vol. 1* (October 1988) reached the top 5 and went multi-platinum
Orbison died of a heart attack on December 6, 1988, just weeks after the album's release
Second album cheekily titled *Vol. 3* (1990), recorded as a four-piece after Orbison's death
Dylan won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 2016 — the only Nobel laureate in a rock supergroup

items.prompt: Which supergroup played Woodstock in 1969 as only their second live performance, with members drawn from the Byrds, Buffalo Springfield, and the Hollies?
items.explanation:
David Crosby (Byrds), Stephen Stills (Buffalo Springfield), Graham Nash (Hollies), Neil Young (Buffalo Springfield)
Woodstock 1969 was only their second live performance; Stills told the crowd 'We're scared shitless'
'Suite: Judy Blue Eyes' — written by Stills about singer Judy Collins — was their opening song at Woodstock
Young joined shortly after the debut CSN album; he skipped most of the acoustic set at Woodstock
*Déjà Vu* (1970) with Young hit #1 and went quadruple platinum
'Ohio' (1970), written by Young, protested the Kent State shootings and was recorded and released within weeks
Notorious for internal feuds — the group split and reformed repeatedly across five decades

items.prompt: Which supergroup combined the vocalist of Soundgarden with the instrumentalists of Rage Against the Machine, active 2001–2007?
items.explanation:
Chris Cornell (vocals, ex-Soundgarden) with Tom Morello (guitar), Tim Commerford (bass), Brad Wilk (drums) from Rage Against the Machine
Active 2001–2007; released three studio albums and sold over 8 million records worldwide
Self-titled debut (2002) went triple platinum; 'Like a Stone' became their biggest hit
First American rock band to perform an open-air concert in Cuba (2005)
Cornell left in February 2007 citing 'irresolvable personality conflicts and musical differences'
Briefly reunited for the Anti-Inaugural Ball on January 20, 2017; Cornell died four months later
Cornell died by suicide on May 18, 2017, after a Soundgarden concert in Detroit

items.prompt: Which country supergroup, nicknamed 'the Mount Rushmore of country music,' united four outlaw-country legends from 1985 to 1995?
items.explanation:
Johnny Cash, Willie Nelson, Waylon Jennings, Kris Kristofferson — active 1985–1995
Formed after the four were together in Switzerland filming a Johnny Cash Christmas TV special in 1984
Named after their #1 hit 'Highwayman' (1985), written by Jimmy Webb — a ballad about reincarnation
Released three studio albums: two on Columbia Records, one on Liberty Records
Starred together in the 1986 TV movie *Stagecoach*
Nelson is the sole surviving member; Jennings died 2002, Cash died 2003, Kristofferson died 2024
Often called 'the Mount Rushmore of country music' by PBS and the press

items.prompt: Which 1969 supergroup featuring members of the Yardbirds, Traffic, and a prior supergroup released only one self-titled album before disbanding?
items.explanation:
Eric Clapton (guitar), Steve Winwood (keyboards/vocals, ex-Traffic), Ginger Baker (drums, ex-Cream), Ric Grech (bass, ex-Family)
Formed in early 1969; released one self-titled album in August 1969 — it topped charts on both sides of the Atlantic
Album sold over 500,000 copies in its first month despite the band lasting only a few months
Controversial album cover featured a topless 11-year-old girl holding a metallic airplane; later pressings used an alternate photo
Clapton and Baker had just left their prior supergroup (active 1966–1968) months earlier
Disbanded in October 1969 after a brief summer tour — one of the shortest-lived supergroups in rock history
Winwood returned to Traffic; Clapton formed Derek and the Dominos

items.prompt: Which 1981 duet, improvised during a studio session in Montreux, features a bass line later sampled by Vanilla Ice for 'Ice Ice Baby'?
alternates: Under Pressure by Queen and David Bowie
items.explanation:
Recorded by Queen and David Bowie at Mountain Studios in Montreux, Switzerland (1981)
Largely improvised — Bowie was visiting the studio while Queen was recording *Hot Space*
Reached #1 in the UK; Queen's first UK chart-topper since 'Bohemian Rhapsody' (1975)
Iconic bass line (played by John Deacon) was sampled without credit by Vanilla Ice on 'Ice Ice Baby' (1990)
Vanilla Ice initially denied sampling, claiming the melodies were different by one note; he later admitted it was a joke
Queen and Bowie sued and received songwriting credits on 'Ice Ice Baby' through a private settlement
The bass line was voted the best in popular music history by *Stylus* magazine (2005)

items.prompt: Which 1982 duet about racial harmony, pairing an ex-Beatle with a Motown legend, spent seven weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100?
items.explanation:
Recorded by Paul McCartney and Stevie Wonder; released March 1982
Spent seven weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 — the longest chart-topping run of McCartney's post-Beatles career
Recorded at George Martin's AIR Studios in Montserrat in early 1981
Also reached #1 in the UK; fourth-biggest hit in the U.S. for 1982
Made Wonder the first solo artist with a #1 single in three consecutive decades (1960s, 1970s, 1980s)
McCartney and Wonder filmed their music video parts separately due to conflicting schedules
Frequently cited as one of the most critically derided #1 hits despite its massive commercial success

items.prompt: Which 1976 duet, written under the pseudonyms 'Ann Orson' and 'Carte Blanche,' became the first UK #1 for both of its performers?
items.explanation:
Recorded by Elton John and Kiki Dee; released June 1976
First #1 in the UK for both artists — topped the chart for six weeks
Also hit #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 for four weeks, making it Elton John's sixth American chart-topper
Written by John and Bernie Taupin under the pseudonyms 'Ann Orson' and 'Carte Blanche'
Intended as an affectionate pastiche of Motown duets, particularly Marvin Gaye and Tammi Terrell
Revived in popularity after appearing in a karaoke scene in the 2003 film *Elf*
Kiki Dee (born Pauline Matthews) was the first British female artist signed to Motown's Tamla label

items.prompt: Which 1983 country-pop duet, written by the Bee Gees and named after a Hemingway novel, topped the Billboard Hot 100, Country, and Adult Contemporary charts simultaneously?
items.explanation:
Recorded by Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton; released August 1983
Written by Barry, Robin, and Maurice Gibb (Bee Gees) for Rogers's album *Eyes That See in the Dark*
Topped the Billboard Hot 100, Country, and Adult Contemporary charts simultaneously
Title taken from Ernest Hemingway's posthumous 1970 novel of the same name
Originally written in an R&B style that wasn't working; adding Parton's vocals transformed the song
Robin Gibb jokingly claimed it was written for Marvin Gaye, but the timeline doesn't support this
Became the signature Rogers–Parton collaboration and a karaoke staple for decades
items.cardFront: Rogers & Parton, written by the Bee Gees (1983)

items.prompt: Which 1981 duet spent nine weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100, making it the most successful duet of the rock era at the time?
items.explanation:
Recorded by Diana Ross and Lionel Richie; released in 1981
Spent nine weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 — the longest-running duet chart-topper of the rock era at the time
Also topped the R&B and Adult Contemporary charts
Written and co-produced by Richie as the theme for the 1981 Franco Zeffirelli film starring Brooke Shields
Surpassed the Everly Brothers' 'Wake Up Little Susie' (1957) as the most successful duet on the Hot 100
Billboard later named it the greatest duet of all time
Richie was still a member of the Commodores when he recorded it; it helped launch his solo career

items.prompt: Which 1983 collaboration between the King of Pop and an ex-Beatle preceded a business deal over a music catalog that ended their friendship?
items.explanation:
Recorded by Paul McCartney and Michael Jackson; released October 1983
Hit #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 — Jackson's sixth American chart-topper
Produced by George Martin; recorded during sessions for McCartney's *Tug of War* (1982)
Their second duet after 'The Girl Is Mine' (1982) from Jackson's *Thriller*
McCartney famously showed Jackson a catalog of his publishing holdings and advised him to invest in music rights
Two years later, Jackson outbid McCartney for the ATV Music catalog containing 251 Beatles songs for $47.5 million
The catalog purchase strained their friendship; McCartney called it 'a dodgy thing to do'
items.cardFront: McCartney & Jackson, before the catalog dispute (1983)
